instruction:
You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

KEYWORDS:
  STATE     — what the app holds in memory. Plain prose, no code.
  TYPE      — the shape of one entity in a collection. Concepts only, no database types.
  STRUCTURE — what the user sees: regions, labels, inputs, buttons. No CSS, no colors.
  WHEN      — one user action and its result. Trigger → change → feedback.

OUTPUT FORMAT — follow exactly:

PART_ID: Part Name
STATE: Section Name
MARKER: snake_case_marker
INTENT: one short phrase
---
Two to four plain sentences.

RULES:
- PART_ID and section names are natural readable titles.
- Never use "global", "app", "main", "default" as a PART_ID.
- Every app needs 2-4 parts. Group STATE+TYPE together; STRUCTURE and WHEN can share a part.
- The line after PART_ID must be STATE / TYPE / STRUCTURE / WHEN followed by a colon and a name.
- NEVER write STATE: WHEN or STATE: STRUCTURE — those are keywords, not names.
- MARKER is always snake_case only.
- No bullet points anywhere. Prose only.
- No code syntax, no type annotations, no database terms.
- Begin output immediately with the first PART_ID line.
input:
add a recipe rating system

EXISTING MID:
  PART [Recipes] status:verified
    STATE: Recipe Collection — holds all recipes
    TYPE: Recipe — shape of one recipe
  PART [Interface] status:draft
    STRUCTURE: Recipe Browser — what the user sees
    STRUCTURE: Recipe Detail — full recipe view
    WHEN: Add Recipe — user creates a new recipe
    WHEN: Filter Recipes — user narrows the list
FOCUS: Modify part [Recipes].
output:
PART_ID: Recipes
TYPE: Recipe
MARKER: recipe_type
INTENT: shape of one recipe
---
A recipe has a title, a category, a block of text describing the ingredients, a block of text describing the preparation method, a serving count, and a personal rating from one to five stars. The rating starts unset.

instruction:
You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

KEYWORDS:
  STATE     — what the app holds in memory. Plain prose, no code.
  TYPE      — the shape of one entity in a collection. Concepts only, no database types.
  STRUCTURE — what the user sees: regions, labels, inputs, buttons. No CSS, no colors.
  WHEN      — one user action and its result. Trigger → change → feedback.

OUTPUT FORMAT — follow exactly:

PART_ID: Part Name
STATE: Section Name
MARKER: snake_case_marker
INTENT: one short phrase
---
Two to four plain sentences.

RULES:
- PART_ID and section names are natural readable titles.
- Never use "global", "app", "main", "default" as a PART_ID.
- Every app needs 2-4 parts. Group STATE+TYPE together; STRUCTURE and WHEN can share a part.
- The line after PART_ID must be STATE / TYPE / STRUCTURE / WHEN followed by a colon and a name.
- NEVER write STATE: WHEN or STATE: STRUCTURE — those are keywords, not names.
- MARKER is always snake_case only.
- No bullet points anywhere. Prose only.
- No code syntax, no type annotations, no database terms.
- Begin output immediately with the first PART_ID line.
output:
PART_ID: Counter
STATE: Counter State
MARKER: word_counter_state
INTENT: holds input text and word frequencies
---
The app holds the raw text entered by the user and a computed map of each unique word to its count. The map is recalculated whenever the text changes.

PART_ID: Counter
STRUCTURE: Counter Layout
MARKER: word_counter_layout
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page has a large text area where the user pastes or types text. Below it a table shows each unique word and its frequency, sorted from most to least frequent. A summary line above the table shows the total word count and unique word count.

PART_ID: Counter
WHEN: Analyze Text
MARKER: analyze_text
INTENT: user edits the input text
---
When the user types or pastes text into the text area, the word frequency table recalculates in real time. Punctuation is stripped before counting. Words are compared case-insensitively.

output:
PART_ID: Data
STATE: Dashboard Data
MARKER: dashboard_data
INTENT: holds the summary data displayed on the dashboard
---
The app holds a set of summary metrics and a list of recent activity entries. The metrics and activity load when the page opens.

PART_ID: Data
TYPE: Activity Entry
MARKER: activity_entry_type
INTENT: shape of one recent activity item
---
An activity entry has a description, a timestamp, and a category.

PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Dashboard Layout
MARKER: dashboard_layout
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page shows a row of metric cards at the top, each displaying a number and a label. Below the cards is a recent activity list showing the latest entries with their descriptions and timestamps.

instruction:
You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

KEYWORDS:
  STATE     — what the app holds in memory. Plain prose, no code.
  TYPE      — the shape of one entity in a collection. Concepts only, no database types.

MARKER: snake_case_marker

input: build a pomodoro timer with task list
output:
PART_ID: Pomodoro
STATE: Session State
MARKER: session_state
INTENT: tracks the current pomodoro session
---
The app holds the current phase which is either work or break, the remaining time, and the count of completed pomodoros. Work phases last 25 minutes. Short breaks last 5 minutes. After four pomodoros a long break of 15 minutes replaces the short break.

PART_ID: Tasks
STATE: Task List
MARKER: pomo_task_list
INTENT: holds tasks to work through
---
The app holds a list of tasks linked to the pomodoro session. Each task can be marked complete. The list persists across timer resets.

PART_ID: Tasks
TYPE: Pomodoro Task
MARKER: pomo_task_type
INTENT: shape of one task
---
A task has a title, a completion status, and a count of how many pomodoros were spent on it.

PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Pomodoro Layout
MARKER: pomodoro_layout
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page shows a large timer display with the phase label above it. Start, Pause, and Reset buttons sit below. The completed pomodoro count appears as dot icons below the buttons. The task list occupies the lower half of the page with an Add Task input at the bottom.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Start Session
MARKER: start_session
INTENT: user begins a work phase
---
When the user clicks Start, the timer begins counting down from the work phase duration. When the timer reaches zero a notification appears and the break phase begins automatically.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Complete Pomodoro
MARKER: complete_pomodoro
INTENT: a full work session finishes
---
When a work phase timer reaches zero, the completed count increments by one and the timer switches to the appropriate break phase. The dot indicators update immediately.

input:
add a quiz leaderboard

EXISTING MID:
  PART [Quiz] status:verified
    STATE: Quiz State — tracks quiz progress
    TYPE: Question — shape of one question
  PART [Interface] status:draft
    STRUCTURE: Quiz Layout — what the user sees during the quiz
    STRUCTURE: Results View — what the user sees after finishing
    WHEN: Answer Question — user selects an answer
    WHEN: Restart Quiz — user starts over
VERIFIED PARTS — do not modify [Quiz].
FOCUS: Modify part [Interface].
output:
PART_ID: Interface
STATE: Leaderboard State
MARKER: leaderboard_state
INTENT: holds past quiz scores
---
The interface holds a list of past quiz results. Each result has a player name, a score, and a date.

PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Leaderboard View
MARKER: leaderboard_view
INTENT: shows past scores to the user
---
The leaderboard view shows a table of past results sorted by score from highest to lowest. Each row shows the player name, score, and date. A Back button returns to the quiz. The leaderboard is accessible from the results view after completing the quiz.

input:
add a habit archive feature

EXISTING MID:
  PART [Habits] status:verified
    STATE: Habit State — holds habits and completion records
    TYPE: Habit — shape of one habit
  PART [Interface] status:draft
    STRUCTURE: Habit Dashboard — what the user sees
    WHEN: Check Habit — user marks a habit complete
    WHEN: Add Habit — user creates a new habit
VERIFIED PARTS — do not modify [Habits].
FOCUS: Modify part [Interface].
output:
PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Archive Habit
MARKER: archive_habit
INTENT: user hides a habit without deleting it
---
When the user clicks the archive button on a habit row, the habit is moved out of the active list and into an archived state. An Archived tab at the top of the dashboard shows all archived habits. Archived habits do not appear in the daily checklist. A restore button in the archived view returns a habit to the active list.

MARKER: recipe_type

---
A recipe has a title, a category, a list of tags, a block of text describing the ingredients, a block of text describing the preparation method, and a serving count. Tags are short descriptive words such as quick or vegetarian.

You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

output:
PART_ID: Links
STATE: Link Store
MARKER: link_store
INTENT: holds all shortened links
---
The app holds a map of short codes to original URLs. Each short code is generated from the original URL. The store starts empty.

PART_ID: Links
STRUCTURE: Shortener Layout
MARKER: shortener_layout
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page has a URL input field and a Shorten button at the top. Below it the shortened URL is displayed as a clickable link with a copy button beside it. A table below shows all previously shortened links with their short codes and original URLs.

PART_ID: Links
WHEN: Shorten URL
MARKER: shorten_url
INTENT: user creates a short link
---
When the user pastes a URL and clicks Shorten, a short code is generated and stored alongside the original URL. The shortened link appears below the input. Clicking the shortened link opens the original URL in a new tab.

You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

input: build a simple social feed with likes and comments
output:
PART_ID: Posts
STATE: Feed State
MARKER: feed_state
INTENT: holds all posts in the feed
---
The app holds a list of posts sorted by time with the newest first. Each post tracks its own like count and comment list.

PART_ID: Posts
TYPE: Feed Post
MARKER: feed_post_type
INTENT: shape of one feed post
---
A post has an author name, a text body, a timestamp, a like count, and a list of comments.

PART_ID: Posts
TYPE: Comment
MARKER: comment_type
INTENT: shape of one comment
---
A comment has an author name, a text body, and a timestamp.

PART_ID: Feed
STRUCTURE: Feed Layout
MARKER: feed_layout
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page shows a compose area at the top where the user can write a new post. Below it the feed scrolls vertically showing post cards. Each card shows the author, body, timestamp, a like button with count, and a comment button with count. Clicking the comment button expands a comment thread below the post.

PART_ID: Feed
WHEN: Like Post
MARKER: like_post
INTENT: user likes a post
---
When the user clicks the like button on a post, the like count increments by one and the button changes to an active state. Clicking again decrements the count and returns the button to its default state.

PART_ID: Feed
WHEN: Add Comment
MARKER: add_comment
INTENT: user replies to a post
---
When the user types in the comment input below a post and submits, the comment is added to the post's comment list with the current timestamp and appears at the bottom of the thread.

input:
add search to the news site

EXISTING MID:
  PART [Articles] status:verified
    STATE: Article Feed — holds the collection of articles
    TYPE: Article — shape of one article
  PART [Navigation] status:draft
    STRUCTURE: Site Header — what the user sees at the top
    STRUCTURE: Article Feed Layout — the main content area
    WHEN: Open Article — user reads full article
VERIFIED PARTS — do not modify [Articles].
FOCUS: Modify part [Navigation].
output:
PART_ID: Navigation
WHEN: Search Articles
MARKER: search_articles
INTENT: user filters articles by keyword
---
When the user types in the search input in the header, the article feed filters in real time to show only articles whose headline or summary contains the typed text. Clearing the search restores the full feed.

- No bullet points anywhere. Prose only.

- Begin output immediately with the first PART_ID line.
input: make a quiz app with multiple choice questions
output:
PART_ID: Quiz
STATE: Quiz State
MARKER: quiz_state
INTENT: tracks quiz progress
---
The app holds a list of questions, the index of the current question, the number of correct answers, and a finished status. The finished status is false until all questions are answered.

PART_ID: Quiz
TYPE: Question
MARKER: question_type
INTENT: shape of one question
---
A question has a prompt, a list of four answer options, and the index of the correct answer.

PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Quiz Layout
MARKER: quiz_layout
INTENT: what the user sees during the quiz
---
The page shows the current question number and total at the top, the question prompt in large text, and four answer buttons stacked vertically. A progress bar at the top reflects the proportion of questions answered.

PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Results View
MARKER: results_view
INTENT: what the user sees after finishing
---
The results view shows the final score as a fraction and a percentage, a brief performance message, and a Restart button.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Answer Question
MARKER: answer_question
INTENT: user selects an answer
---
When the user clicks an answer button, the correct answer highlights in green and any incorrect selection highlights in red. After a short pause the next question loads. If this was the last question the results view appears instead.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Restart Quiz
MARKER: restart_quiz
INTENT: user starts over
---
When the user clicks Restart, the current question resets to the first, the correct answer count resets to zero, and the quiz layout replaces the results view.

You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

output:
PART_ID: Quotes
STATE: Quote Collection
MARKER: quote_collection
INTENT: holds the list of quotes
---
The app holds a fixed list of quotes. Each quote has a text and an author. The current displayed quote is selected randomly from the list.

PART_ID: Quotes
TYPE: Quote
MARKER: quote_type
INTENT: shape of one quote
---
A quote has a text body and an author name.

PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Quote Display
MARKER: quote_display
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page shows one quote at a time in large centered text with the author name below it in smaller text. A New Quote button sits below the attribution.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Get New Quote
MARKER: get_new_quote
INTENT: user requests a different quote
---
When the user clicks New Quote, a different quote is selected at random from the list. If the same quote would be selected again, another is chosen instead.

instruction:
You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

KEYWORDS:
  STATE     — what the app holds in memory. Plain prose, no code.
  TYPE      — the shape of one entity in a collection. Concepts only, no database types.
  STRUCTURE — what the user sees: regions, labels, inputs, buttons. No CSS, no colors.
  WHEN      — one user action and its result. Trigger → change → feedback.

OUTPUT FORMAT — follow exactly:

PART_ID: Part Name
STATE: Section Name
MARKER: snake_case_marker
INTENT: one short phrase
---
Two to four plain sentences.

RULES:
- PART_ID and section names are natural readable titles.
- Never use "global", "app", "main", "default" as a PART_ID.
- Every app needs 2-4 parts. Group STATE+TYPE together; STRUCTURE and WHEN can share a part.
- The line after PART_ID must be STATE / TYPE / STRUCTURE / WHEN followed by a colon and a name.
- NEVER write STATE: WHEN or STATE: STRUCTURE — those are keywords, not names.
- MARKER is always snake_case only.
- No bullet points anywhere. Prose only.
- No code syntax, no type annotations, no database terms.
- Begin output immediately with the first PART_ID line.
input:
add a mood chart to the journal

EXISTING MID:
  PART [Journal] status:verified
    STATE: Journal State — holds all journal entries
    TYPE: Journal Entry — shape of one journal entry
  PART [Interface] status:draft
    STRUCTURE: Journal Layout — what the user sees
    WHEN: Save Entry — user saves the current entry
    WHEN: Browse Past Entries — user opens a past entry
VERIFIED PARTS — do not modify [Journal].
FOCUS: Modify part [Interface].
output:
PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Journal Layout
MARKER: journal_layout
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page has a sidebar listing past entry dates with their mood icons and a main area showing the selected entry. A mood trend chart at the top of the sidebar displays the last thirty days of mood ratings as a line graph. The current day's entry is shown by default with an editable body area and a mood selector.

instruction:
You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

KEYWORDS:
  STATE     — what the app holds in memory. Plain prose, no code.
  TYPE      — the shape of one entity in a collection. Concepts only, no database types.
  STRUCTURE — what the user sees: regions, labels, inputs, buttons. No CSS, no colors.
  WHEN      — one user action and its result. Trigger → change → feedback.

OUTPUT FORMAT — follow exactly:

PART_ID: Part Name
STATE: Section Name
MARKER: snake_case_marker
INTENT: one short phrase
---
Two to four plain sentences.

RULES:
- PART_ID and section names are natural readable titles.
- Never use "global", "app", "main", "default" as a PART_ID.
- Every app needs 2-4 parts. Group STATE+TYPE together; STRUCTURE and WHEN can share a part.
- The line after PART_ID must be STATE / TYPE / STRUCTURE / WHEN followed by a colon and a name.
- NEVER write STATE: WHEN or STATE: STRUCTURE — those are keywords, not names.
- MARKER is always snake_case only.
- No bullet points anywhere. Prose only.
- No code syntax, no type annotations, no database terms.
- Begin output immediately with the first PART_ID line.
output:
PART_ID: Streaks
STATE: Streak State
MARKER: streak_state
INTENT: holds habits and daily completion data
---
The app holds a list of tracked habits and a calendar record of which habits were completed on each date. Streaks are calculated from consecutive completed days up to today.

PART_ID: Streaks
TYPE: Tracked Habit
MARKER: tracked_habit_type
INTENT: shape of one tracked habit
---
A tracked habit has a name, a current streak count, and a longest streak count. Both counts are derived from the completion calendar.

PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Streak Dashboard
MARKER: streak_dashboard
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page shows a row of habit cards. Each card shows the habit name, the current streak as a large number with a flame icon, and the all-time longest streak below it. A small calendar grid shows the last 14 days with completed days marked. An Add Habit button sits at the top of the page.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Log Completion
MARKER: log_completion
INTENT: user marks a habit complete for today
---
When the user clicks the check button on a habit card, today is marked complete for that habit. The streak count increments if yesterday was also complete. The calendar grid updates immediately.

instruction:
You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

KEYWORDS:
  STATE     — what the app holds in memory. Plain prose, no code.
  TYPE      — the shape of one entity in a collection. Concepts only, no database types.
  STRUCTURE — what the user sees: regions, labels, inputs, buttons. No CSS, no colors.
  WHEN      — one user action and its result. Trigger → change → feedback.

OUTPUT FORMAT — follow exactly:

PART_ID: Part Name
STATE: Section Name
MARKER: snake_case_marker
INTENT: one short phrase
---
Two to four plain sentences.

RULES:
- PART_ID and section names are natural readable titles.
- Never use "global", "app", "main", "default" as a PART_ID.
- Every app needs 2-4 parts. Group STATE+TYPE together; STRUCTURE and WHEN can share a part.
- The line after PART_ID must be STATE / TYPE / STRUCTURE / WHEN followed by a colon and a name.
- NEVER write STATE: WHEN or STATE: STRUCTURE — those are keywords, not names.
- MARKER is always snake_case only.
- No bullet points anywhere. Prose only.
- No code syntax, no type annotations, no database terms.
- Begin output immediately with the first PART_ID line.
output:
PART_ID: Articles
STATE: Article Feed
MARKER: article_feed
INTENT: holds the collection of articles
---
The app holds a list of articles. Each article starts as a draft. The feed shows published articles only, sorted by publication date with the newest first.

PART_ID: Articles
TYPE: Article
MARKER: article_type
INTENT: shape of one article
---
An article has a headline, a summary, a full body, an author name, a publication date, and a status that is either draft or published. The status is draft by default.

PART_ID: Navigation
STRUCTURE: Site Header
MARKER: site_header
INTENT: what the user sees at the top
---
The page has a header with the site name on the left and a navigation bar with category links on the right. A search input sits at the far right of the header.

PART_ID: Navigation
STRUCTURE: Article Feed Layout
MARKER: feed_layout
INTENT: the main content area
---
The main area shows article cards in a vertical list. Each card displays the headline, a one-line summary, the author name, and the publication date. Clicking a card opens the full article view.

PART_ID: Navigation
WHEN: Open Article
MARKER: open_article
INTENT: user reads full article
---
When the user clicks an article card, the feed view is replaced by the full article view showing the headline, author, date, and full body. A back button returns to the feed.

instruction:
You are a Mid compiler. Mid is a plain-English design language between a user idea and running code.

KEYWORDS:
  STATE     — what the app holds in memory. Plain prose, no code.
  TYPE      — the shape of one entity in a collection. Concepts only, no database types.
  STRUCTURE — what the user sees: regions, labels, inputs, buttons. No CSS, no colors.
  WHEN      — one user action and its result. Trigger → change → feedback.

OUTPUT FORMAT — follow exactly:

PART_ID: Part Name
STATE: Section Name
MARKER: snake_case_marker
INTENT: one short phrase
---
Two to four plain sentences.

RULES:
- PART_ID and section names are natural readable titles.
- Never use "global", "app", "main", "default" as a PART_ID.
- Every app needs 2-4 parts. Group STATE+TYPE together; STRUCTURE and WHEN can share a part.
- The line after PART_ID must be STATE / TYPE / STRUCTURE / WHEN followed by a colon and a name.
- NEVER write STATE: WHEN or STATE: STRUCTURE — those are keywords, not names.
- MARKER is always snake_case only.
- No bullet points anywhere. Prose only.
- No code syntax, no type annotations, no database terms.
- Begin output immediately with the first PART_ID line.
output:
PART_ID: Notes
STATE: Note Collection
MARKER: note_collection
INTENT: holds all notes in memory
---
The app holds a list of notes. Each note has a title and a body. The list starts empty.

PART_ID: Notes
TYPE: Note
MARKER: note_type
INTENT: shape of one note
---
A note has a title, a body of text, and a creation timestamp. The title is required and the body can be empty.

PART_ID: Interface
STRUCTURE: Notes Layout
MARKER: notes_layout
INTENT: what the user sees
---
The page has a sidebar listing all note titles and a main area showing the selected note. A New Note button sits at the top of the sidebar. The main area contains an editable title field and an editable body area.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Create Note
MARKER: create_note
INTENT: user creates a new note
---
When the user clicks New Note, a blank note is added to the list and selected immediately. The title field receives focus so the user can start typing.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Edit Note
MARKER: edit_note
INTENT: user edits the selected note
---
When the user types in the title or body field, the selected note updates in real time. The sidebar reflects the new title immediately.

PART_ID: Interface
WHEN: Delete Note
MARKER: delete_note
INTENT: user removes a note
---
When the user clicks the Delete button on the selected note, it is removed from the list and the next note is selected. If no notes remain the main area shows an empty state message.
